coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform and logical device based on polydopamine nanoparticles and carbonized polymer dots for visual and point-of-care testing of glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Xucan
Bai, Fujuan
Ye, Heng
Zhao, Hanqing
Zhao, Longshan
Xiong, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: As a crucial biothiol, glutathione (GSH) plays a key role in the organisms. Monitoring GSH level is of great significance for disease diagnosis and biomedical research. In this work, polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles-red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (r-CPDs) based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was constructed and employed for GSH assay. Dopamine (DA) could be oxidized by cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets and further polymerized into PDA nanoparticles with green fluorescence. However, in the presence of GSH, CoOOH nanosheets were reduced and decomposed, which prevented the production of PDA nanoparticles. In the sensing system, green-emitting PDA nanoparticles were employed as a response unit and r-CPDs were used as an internal reference unit. With the addition of GSH, the green fluorescence of PDA nanoparticles decreased as well as the red fluorescence of system remained relatively stable. Importantly, a distinct fluorescence color evolution from green to red was presented with a serious of GSH concentrations. Based on this, a portable smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity analytical method was developed to achieve the on-site visual detection of GSH. Both the established ratiometric fluorescence and ratiometric chromaticity sensing methods for GSH assay have the merits of wide linear range, high sensitivity and excellent accuracy, which are suitable for the determination of GSH in human serum and exhibit great application potential in rapid and accurate monitoring of the GSH levels in clinical. Moreover, an ingenious logical device reflecting GSH levels was designed based on the two different fluorescence signals, which provided a new strategy for the intelligent online detection of GSH in complex biological matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polydopamine nanoparticles
Carbonized polymer dots
Ratiometric sensing
Glutathione
Visual
On-site

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (r-CPDs)
Detection of GSH in human serum samples
Characterization of prepared r-CPDs and CoOOH nanosheets
Optical properties and cytotoxicity evaluation of r-CPDs
Feasibility and mechanism study of the constructed sensing strategy
Smartphone-assisted sensing platform for GSH detection
Construction of logic gate device based on dual-fluorescence signals
Detection of GSH in actual samples

Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials
Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020–181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of r-CPDs. TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images; FT-IR spectrum (c); full-scan XPS spectrum (d).
Characterization of CoOOH nanosheets. TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images; UV–vis absorption spectrum (c); FT-IR spectrum (d); full-scan XPS spectrum (e); Co 2p region of XPS analysis (f).

Fluorescence spectra of DA, CoOOH + DA, r-CPDs, CoOOH + DA + r-CPDs, GSH + CoOOH + DA + r-CPDs. Inset: the photographs of PDA nanoparticles under visible light (left) and UV light (right) (a); UV–vis absorption spectrum of DA, CoOOH, CoOOH + DA, excitation and emission spectra of r-CPDs (b); fluorescence decay curves of r-CPDs and r-CPDs + PDA nanoparticles (c).

Fluorescence emission spectra of ratiometric fluorescence sensing systems with different concentrations of GSH (a and b); F490/F600 varies with the GSH concentration (1–100 μM) (c); the CIE chromaticity coordinates of ratiometric fluorescence sensing systems with different concentrations of GSH (d).

Fluorescence photographs of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing systems in the presence of a series of concentrations of GSH under 365 nm UV lamp illumination (a); the linear relationship between the GSH concentration and the values of G/R (b).

Truth table of logic gate (a); definition pattern diagram of the logic gate (b); normalized fluorescence intensity versus different GSH concentrations ranging from 1 to 100 μM (c); logic analytical results for GSH assay (d).
The PDA nanoparticles and r-CPDs based ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity sensing platforms as well as logic gate device were employed for GSH assay.
Comparison of the proposed methods with other reported methods for GSH assay.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: M-MoS2QDs/L-Cys-BODIPY: Monolayer MoS2 quantum dots/l-cystine-linked boron-dipyrromethene.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for the determination of GSH in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform and logical device based on polydopamine nanoparticles and carbonized polymer dots for visual and point-of-care testing of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.
Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a crucial biothiol, glutathione (GSH) plays a key role in the organisms. Monitoring GSH level is of great significance for disease diagnosis and biomedical research. In this work, polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles-red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (r-CPDs) based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was constructed and employed for GSH assay. Dopamine (DA) could be oxidized by cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets and further polymerized into PDA nanoparticles with green fluorescence. However, in the presence of GSH, CoOOH nanosheets were reduced and decomposed, which prevented the production of PDA nanoparticles. In the sensing system, green-emitting PDA nanoparticles were employed as a response unit and r-CPDs were used as an internal reference unit. With the addition of GSH, the green fluorescence of PDA nanoparticles decreased as well as the red fluorescence of system remained relatively stable. Importantly, a distinct fluorescence color evolution from green to red was presented with a serious of GSH concentrations. Based on this, a portable smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity analytical method was developed to achieve the on-site visual detection of GSH. Both the established ratiometric fluorescence and ratiometric chromaticity sensing methods for GSH assay have the merits of wide linear range, high sensitivity and excellent accuracy, which are suitable for the determination of GSH in human serum and exhibit great application potential in rapid and accurate monitoring of the GSH levels in clinical. Moreover, an ingenious logical device reflecting GSH levels was designed based on the two different fluorescence signals, which provided a new strategy for the intelligent online detection of GSH in complex biological matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a vital non-protein thiol, which is ubiquitous in the human body and plays significant role in various physiological and pathological functions [1–3]. As an important endogenous antioxidant, GSH can scavenge free radicals, keep intracellular redox homeostasis, regulate immune response and participate in many crucial metabolic processes [4–6]. Moreover, GSH is regarded as an important biomarker reflecting human health and the abnormal levels of GSH in the biological systems are greatly associated with many diseases, such as neurodegenerative diseases, HIV infection, liver injury and cancer [1,3,5,7]. Thus, exploiting a convenient, reliable and sensitive method to monitor the GSH levels in biological systems has great meaning for clinical preliminary diagnosis and treatment of related diseases. At present, various analytical techniques for the determination of GSH have been reported including high performance liquid chromatography [8], electrochemistry [9], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [10] and fluorometry [11–13]. It is no doubt that these methods can achieve satisfying and convincing results, but the application of some methods was restricted due to tedious operations, time consuming and expensive equipments. Owing to the advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity, short analysis time and easy visualization, the fluorometry has attracted increasing attention in analysis field.

Currently, a novel two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterial, cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets have been widely employed for fluorescence sensing systems due to their unique merits, such as facile preparation, good water dispersity, broad UV absorption and excellent oxidization ability [14–16]. Up to now, it has been reported that CoOOH nanosheets were used as fluorescence quencher and recognition unit to construct fluorescence sensing platforms for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) [16], alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [17], acetylcholinesterase (AChE) [18], etc. Polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles are a kind of intrinsic fluorescent nanomaterial, which can be produced under oxidative stress or alkaline conditions [19]. Recently, the application of PDA nanoparticles in chemical analysis and biosensing field has obtained great research interest resulting from the excellent biocompatibility and physicochemical properties [19,20]. Liu et al. utilized the PDA nanoparticles for ALP and organophosphate pesticides detection [21]. Lin et al. designed a novel immunosensing strategy based on PDA nanoparticles for the detection of alpha-fetoprotein [20]. However, in fluorescence assay, single fluorescence signal response is susceptible to interferences from environment and instrument conditions. Fortunately, the ratiometric fluorescence assay using the ratio of fluorescence signals at two different wavelengths as the response signals has self-calibration characteristic, which can overcome the above interferences, thus achieving efficient and sensitive detection of analytes in complex matrices [19,22].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a typical of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial, have been widely concerned since they were found and reported [23]. Due to the distinctive physical, optical and biological properties including small size, high water solubility, excellent photostability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, CDs have been extensively used in the research fields of sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and disease diagnosis and therapy [24–27]. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), an emerging classification of CDs, have idiographic polymer/carbon hybrid structures [28,29], which can be prepared via incomplete carbonization of polymer clusters [29]. More importantly, as a promising fluorescent material, they have good application prospects in the fields of biochemical sensing and bioimaging. At present, the CDs prepared by most methods emit blue fluorescence, which greatly limits their application in biomedicine, because biological substrates often show blue autofluorescence under ultraviolet light [28]. CDs with long emission wavelengths (red or near-infrared) can enhance tissue penetration and reduce the background interference from autofluorescence of biological matrices, but the preparation of red or near-infrared CDs is often faced with many problems, such as low quantum yield, poor biocompatibility and complex preparation processes [28]. Therefore, the synthesis of red CDs with excellent optical properties and high biocompatibility is of great value in the fields of biological imaging, biosensing and biomedical therapy.

Although most quantitative analysis can achieve high sensitivity and accuracy with the assistance of instruments and technicians, they are not suitable for real-time and on-site analysis. Smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity analysis as one of the promising detection methods has been employed for quick and precise on-site detection of a variety of analytes, which is mainly attributed to the portability, image capture and recognition capabilities of smartphone [30–32]. Smartphone as a portable device can not only take fluorescent color photos of different systems, but also convert the color signals into RGB values in virtue of a color recognizer APP to reflect the concentrations of the analytes [33]. RGB color is one of the most widely used color systems and it contains almost all colors perceived by human naked eyes [34]. Compared with the visual analysis with naked eyes, image chromaticity analysis based on smartphones can provide more excellent sensitivity and accuracy since naked eyes can not distinguish the slight color change [31,34]. Moreover, dual-emission fluorescence system exhibits a noticeable color evolution toward different levels of analytes, while single fluorescence signal sensing only reveals a brightness variation [30]. As a result, the fabrication of smartphone-assisted sensing analysis method based on the ratiometric fluorescence strategy is more conducive to on-site monitoring.

Herein, PDA nanoparticles and r-CPDs based ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity sensing platforms as well as an advanced logic gate analytical strategy were constructed and employed for GSH assay (Scheme 1 ). r-CPDs with favorable water solubility and biocompatibility were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. CoOOH nanosheets could oxidize DA and promote the generation of PDA nanoparticles that emit green fluorescence at 490 nm. The green fluorescence decreased with increasing GSH concentration because the CoOOH nanosheets were consumed by the redox reaction with GSH, whereas the red fluorescence of system was basically constant. Simultaneously, the fluorescent color of systems gradually varied from green to red with the addition of GSH and the fluorescent color images can be taken via a smartphone and further converted into RGB values through the installed color recognizer App. Therefore, the ratios of fluorescence intensity or chromaticity can be utilized to quantitatively analyze GSH. In particular, the developed ratiometric chromaticity assay possesses the virtues of simple operation, cost saving and excellent sensitivity, which is suitable for the on-site visual monitoring of GSH levels in complicated matrices.
The chemicals and instruments used in the experiment are detailed in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (r-CPDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The r-CPDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal approach referring to the previous study [35]. p-PD (0.1081 g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL), followed by adding sulfuric acid (55 μL). The obtained mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated in an oven (200 °C, 2 h). After cooling, the resulting product was purified through adding ethanol (v product:v ethanol = 4:1). The suspension was vortexed (5 min) and centrifuged (4000 rpm, 5 min) and supernatant was collected. The above procedure was repeated for 4 cycles. Subsequently, the solution was concentrated and dried to obtain the r-CPDs powder. The r-CPDs solution (3.5 mg mL−1) was prepared by dissolving r-CPD powder into ultrapure water.

To assess the cell toxicity of the prepared r-CPDs, the cell viability of rat aortic smooth muscle cells (RASMC), Hepa 1–6 and HCT-116 cells was studied via Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. In viable cells, WST-8 (a water-soluble tetrazolium salt) will be reduced in the presence of electron mediator by dehydrogenases and transformed to a water-soluble orange colored formazan dye. Therefore, the number of living cells can be directly reflected by the amount of formazan produced. In brief, the cells (100 μL, 6000 cell seeds) were cultured in a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h in an incubator (5% CO2, 37 °C). Then, r-CPDs solutions with different concentrations (100 μL) were added and incubated together with cells for 72 h, and then CCK-8 solution (10 μL) was introduced to each well plate. After incubating for 4 h, the absorbance at 450 nm was measured by a microplate reader. The cell viability was calculated as the following equation:
(1)
V i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
[
(
A
s a m p l e
−
A
b l a n k
)
−
(
A
c o n t r o l
−
A
b l a n k
)
]
× 100
where Asample, Ablank and Acontrol are the absorbance at 450 nm of test, blank and control groups.

The CoOOH nanosheets were prepared based on the previous report with some modifications [36]. Firstly, NaOH solution (1 M, 1.5 mL) and CoCl2 solution (10 mM, 50 mL) were mixed and sonicated for 5 min, and then the resultant suspension was centrifuged (4000 rpm, 10 min). Next, the supernatant was discarded and the precipitate was re-dispersed into ultrapure water (50 mL). Subsequently, NaClO solution (1 mL, 0.63 M) was added to the suspension and the mixture was subjected to ultrasonic (20 min) and centrifugation (4000 rpm, 10 min). Afterwards, the obtained precipitate was washed with ultrapure water and ethanol for three times, respectively, and dried at 60 °C for further use.

GSH solutions (200 μL) with different concentrations and CoOOH nanosheets solution (100 μL, 0.1 mg mL−1) were mixed and incubated (25 °C, 10 min). Then, DA solution (100 μL, 50 mM) was added and incubated together (25 °C, 20 min). Afterwards, hydrochloric acid (100 μL, 0.1 M) was introduced to above mixture to end the polymerization reaction. Finally, ultrapure water (200 μL) and r-CPDs solution (70 μL) were added, followed by sufficient mixing. The fluorescence intensity of reaction system was measured at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. Moreover, the fluorescent color of system under 365 nm UV lamp was recorded by a smartphone. Then, photograph color was converted into corresponding RGB values using a color recognizer application installed on the smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the proposed strategy was further explored by the monitoring of GSH levels in actual human serum. The human serum was obtained from the first affiliated hospital of Jilin University (Changchun, Jinlin). The serum samples were diluted 1000-fold for further experiments. A series of concentrations of GSH solutions were introduced in diluted serum to prepare the spiked samples and the samples were analyzed by the same procedures and conditions as the GSH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of prepared r-CPDs and CoOOH nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM and HRTEM images of prepared r-CPDs are shown in Fig. 1 (a and b). It is found that the r-CPDs are spheroidicity with well-resolved crystallinity and lattice spacing of 0.26 nm. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to reflect the surface groups of r-CPDs. In Fig. 1c, the strong peak appeared at 3443 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of O–H or N–H bond [37,38]. The absorption at 1636 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CN/CC/CO [37,38]. The peak at 1400 cm−1 corresponds to the C–N bond stretching vibration [39,40]. The peaks emerged at 1119 cm−1 and 619 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration peaks of CS/SO and C–S, respectively [40,41]. XPS was utilized to analyze the elemental compositions of the prepared r-CPDs. The XPS result of r-CPDs (Fig. 1d) presents four major peaks of S 2p (167 eV), C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (401 eV) and O 1s (531 eV) and the contents of S, C, N and O elements are 2.67%, 80.95%, 5.08% and 11.3%, respectively. The HRXPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S1a) is disassembled into three peaks at 284.2, 284.7 and 285.7 eV, which correspond to CC, C–C and C–N/C–S bonds, respectively [42]. The N 1s HRXPS spectrum (Fig. S1b) contains three peaks at 399.9, 400.8 and 401.5 eV, belonging to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N [39]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1c) depicts two peaks at 530.9 eV and 531.8 eV, which are associated with CO and C–OH/C–O–C [42,43]. Three peaks at 167.2, 168.3 and 169.3 eV are exhibited in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. S1d), indicating the existence of -C-SOx (x = 2, 3, 4) groups [44]. The above results suggested that S, N and O elements have been successfully doped into r-CPDs.

The synthesized CoOOH nanosheets were also characterized and analyzed to reflect the structure and property. From the TEM (Fig. 2 a) and HRTEM (Fig. 2b) images, it can be clearly seen that the CoOOH nanosheets present a regular hexagonal sheet structure. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2c) displays that CoOOH nanosheets possess a broad absorption (250–500 nm) and the characteristic absorption peak appears at around 400 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. 2d) shows an absorption peak at 3427 cm−1, suggesting that the existence of –OH group [36]. The peak emerged at 1630 cm−1 corresponds the typical absorption of the CoO bond [36,45]. In addition, the peak at 576 cm−1 is attributed to the Co–O2- in CoOOH nanosheets [36,45]. The XPS spectrum of CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. 2e) presents two peaks distinct located at 530.0 eV and 779.6 eV, corresponding to O 1s and Co 2p. Moreover, high-resolution XPS of Co 2p (Fig. 2f) displays two peaks derived from Co 2p3/2 (779.5 eV) and Co 2p1/2 (794.5 eV), which are ascribed to the spin orbit split of Co 2p [45,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and cytotoxicity evaluation of r-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis spectrum of r-CPDs (Fig. S2a) shows two characteristic absorption peaks at around 230 nm and 280 nm, which might be ascribed to the π-π∗ electron transitions of the CC and CN bonds. The inset of Fig. S2a displays that the r-CPDs present light pink under daylight and emit red fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. Quantum yield is one of the important optical properties of luminescent materials. Compared with relative quantum yield, absolute quantum yield can more directly reflect the fluorescence performance of materials. The absolute quantum yield is defined as [47,48]:
(2)
Q Y =
∫
L
e m i s s i o n
∫
E
s o l v e n t
− ∫
E
s a m p l e
where QY is the absolute quantum yield, L
emission is the photon numbers of the fluorescence emission of sample, and E
solvent and E
sample are photon numbers of solvent and sample excited by excitation light, respectively. In this study, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of prepared r-CPDs was measured to be 5.2% by a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a calibrated integrating sphere. Subsequently, the fluorescence stability of r-CPDs under different ion intensity, pH and Xe lamp irradiation was further evaluated. In Fig. S2b, the fluorescence signal response of r-CPDs hardly changes in the NaCl solution (0–9 mM), indicating that r-CPDs have good stability in salt solution. Fig. S2c shows the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of r-CPDs and it can obtain that the fluorescence intensity of r-CPDs did not kenspeckle vary in the pH of 3–10. In addition, after irradiation of the r-CPDs solution with the Xe lamp for 120 min, no obvious photobleaching phenomenon was observed (Fig. S2d). Therefore, the prepared r-CPDs have satisfactory chemical stability and photobleaching resistance, which is conducive to the further application of r-CPDs in fluorescence analysis.

To explore the application prospect of r-CPDs in biological matrices, it is imperative to evaluate the toxicity of r-CPDs. The toxicity assay results of r-CPD with a serious of concentrations on different type of cells are displayed in Fig. S3. It can obtain that the cell viability was greater than 80%, when three cells were treated for 72 h with r-CPDs and the concentration of r-CPDs was not greater than 50 μg mL−1, indicating that the concentration of r-CPDs below 50 μg mL−1 has low toxicity and relatively bio-safety. Moreover, it can be seen from the results of cell viability that r-CPDs exhibit different toxicity for the different types of cells. Under the same exposure conditions, the survival rate of Hepa 1–6 cells was higher than that of RASMC and HCT 116 cells, indicating that r-CPDs were low toxicity to Hepa 1–6 cells compared with the other two types of cells. Furthermore, the cell viability of RASMC and HCT 116 cells gradually decreased with the increase of r-CPDs concentration, which suggested that the toxicity of r-CPDs toward RASMC and HCT 116 cells was concentration-dependent. The experiment of cell viability in vitro could preliminary reflect the toxicity of r-CPDs, which is of great significance for further application of r-CPDs in cell imaging and evaluation of the toxicity of r-CPDs in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism study of the constructed sensing strategy
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility and response mechanism of the proposed sensing strategy toward GSH were verified and analyzed by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of related reaction systems. As presented in Fig. 3 a, no significant fluorescence was observed for the DA solution alone, whereas an obvious fluorescence emission was presented at 490 nm when DA was incubated with CoOOH nanosheets. It is because DA could be oxidized and further polymerized into PDA nanoparticles with green fluorescence in the presence of CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. 3a inset) [49–52]. Additionally, compared with UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. 3b), a stronger UV–vis absorption was presented after incubating DA and CoOOH nanosheets (GSH was absent), which is ascrible to the generation of fluorescent PDA nanoparticles. When GSH was introduced into reaction system, CoOOH nanosheets were destructed due to the reducibility of GSH, resulting in the fluorescence intensity at 490 nm significantly decreased (Fig. 3a). As can be seen from Fig. S4, compared with the control (CoOOH nanosheets alone), the CoOOH nanosheets were etched after adding GSH, which further proved that GSH-triggered the dissociation of CoOOH nanosheets. Moreover, the changes of the size distribution and zeta potential of CoOOH nanosheets before and after the reaction with GSH were investigated. The results (Fig. S5) showed that compared with control CoOOH nanosheets (441 nm), the size of CoOOH nanosheets (268 nm) after the reaction with GSH was significantly reduced. According to Fig. S6, when GSH was added into CoOOH nanosheets solution, the zeta potential of CoOOH nanosheets changed from 16.4 mV (CoOOH nanosheets alone) to −7.9 mV (GSH + CoOOH nanosheets). The above results were attributed to the effective redox reaction between GSH and CoOOH nanosheets.

At present, MnO2 based-GSH sensing methods have been reported [51,53]. The sensing mechanisms of MnO2 and CoOOH nanosheets based-GSH sensing systems are similar. MnO2 and CoOOH nanosheets are oxidization nanomaterials and will be reduced in the presence of GSH, which lead to the fluorescence change of systems. However, most of the reported MnO2 based-GSH sensing systems were single signal response, which only present the change of fluorescence brightness of systems and not suitable for visual detection. In order to achieve the sensitive, reliable and visual detection of GSH in complex matrices, r-CPDs with excellent fluorescence performance were used to construct the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for the detection of GSH in this study. As shown in Fig. 3a, r-CPDs exhibited a maximum fluorescence emission at 600 nm. It is worth noting that the PDA nanoparticles with a broad emission spectrum has fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength of r-CPDs. However, compared with r-CPDs alone, no obvious fluorescence enhancement was observed at 600 nm when r-CPDs were added into CoOOH + DA (PDA nanoparticles) system, which may be owing to the quenching effect of PDA nanoparticles on the fluorescence of r-CPDs. Fig. 3b displays the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH + DA (PDA nanoparticles) overlapped partially with the excitation and emission spectra of r-CPDs. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of r-CPDs and r-CPDs + PDA nanoparticles were calculated to be 2.24 ns and 2.22 ns, respectively. It indicated that the fluorescence lifetime of r-CPDs did not change significantly after mixing with PDA nanoparticles. Therefore, the fluorescence quench of r-CPDs by PDA nanoparticles might be owing to inner filter effect (IFE) [54,55].

To ensure the optimal analysis performance of the proposed sensing method, the effects of several vital parameters on the synthesis of PDA nanoparticles were investigated in detail, including the pH, concentrations of CoOOH nanosheets, incubation time and temperature. The pH has a significant effect on the self-polymerization of DA. As depicted in Fig. S7a, the fluorescence intensity at 490 nm (F490) was very weak at acidic and neutral pH, indicating that the self-polymerization of DA has little influence on the fluorescence sensing system at acidic and neutral pH. Therefore, the pH of the system was not adjusted to facilitate the operation. In Fig. S7b, the F490 reached a maximum value when CoOOH nanosheets concentration was 0.1 mg mL−1. However, with further increasing CoOOH nanosheets concentration, the F490 values gradually decreased, which could be ascribed to the quenching effect of remaining CoOOH nanosheets or the generation of PDA nanoparticles with large-size. Fig. S7c shows that the fluorescence response of PDA nanoparticles progressively increased with increasing incubation time from 5 to 20 min, and then declined slightly beyond 20 min. Furthermore, the effect of incubation temperature was presented in Fig. S7d, and the highest fluorescence intensity at 490 nm can be observed at 25 °C. Thus, incubation time and temperature were selected as 20 min and 25 °C, respectively.

To evaluate the sensing capability of proposed ratiometric fluorescence analysis system, the fluorescence emission spectra of the systems with different concentrations of GSH were measured under the optimal detection conditions. It can be seen from the results (Fig. 4 a and b) that the fluorescence emission peak at 490 nm gradually decreased as the concentration of GSH increased, whereas no obvious change was observed for the emission peak at 600 nm. Fig. 4c displays the relationship between the concentration of GSH and the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F490/F600). Good linearity for F490/F600 with GSH concentration in the range of 1–10 μM and 10–100 μM were obtained. The relevant linear equations were expressed as F490/F600 = −0.04249CGSH + 1.69341 (r
2 = 0.9753) and F490/F600 = −0.01188CGSH + 1.34854 (r
2 = 0.9847), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.06 μM based on the calculation of 3σ/slope principle (σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank samples), which is comparable or even lower than previously reported methods for GSH assay (Table 1 ) [3,11–13,56–58] and much less than the GSH levels in biological systems [59]. Subsequently, the obtained fluorescence emission spectra were converted to the CIE chromaticity coordinates, a remarkable color change (green to red) was observed in chromaticity diagram with the increasing GSH concentration from 1 to 100 μM (Fig. 4d), which makes it possible to achieve a visual semi-quantitative detection via the naked eye and quantitative detection by ratiometric chromaticity analysis toward GSH.

To further assess the specificity of the constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensing system toward GSH, the possible interfering substances including metal ions, amino acids and other biomolecules were used and investigated. In Fig. S8, under the identical experimental procedures and detection conditions, expect for AA, Cys and Hcy, no obvious change for F490/F600 value was observed for other interfering substances compared with blank group. The effects of fluorescence intensity resulting from AA, Cys and Hcy could be ascribed to their reducibility. The redox reaction between AA, Cys or Hcy and CoOOH nanosheets could lead to the destruction of CoOOH nanosheets and further cause the fluorescence signal change of the system. It is worth noting that although AA, Cys and Hcy have effects on the fluorescence response of the system, the levels of AA, Cys and Hcy are much lower than that of GSH (millimolar) in the normal biological systems [2,12,59]. Moreover, in this study, the established sensing system is suitable for the analysis of GSH at micromolar level, so it is essential to dilute the samples appropriately before the determination of GSH in biological samples. Therefore, the influences of extremely low levels of AA, Cys and Hcy in the diluted samples on the determination of GSH can be tolerated and accepted. Consequently, the established ratiometric fluorescence sensing system possesses desirable selectivity for the detection of GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted sensing platform for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Although the ratiometric fluorescence sensing method can obtain accurate and reliable analysis results by fluorophotometer, it is not suitable for the on-site analysis of target analytes due to expensive and bulky equipment. On the other hand, it is difficult to distinguish slight color variation by the naked eye, so satisfactory accuracy cannot be achieved by visual with the naked eye. Accordingly, smartphone was employed as a portable analysis instrument for the on-site detection of GSH. The fluorescence color of ratiometric fluorescence system under 365 nm UV lamp can be recorded through taking photos with a smartphone. The obtained photograph color could be transformed into RGB values via a color recognizer APP. In Fig. 5 a, as the GSH concentration increased, the color evolution of the fluorescence system from green to red, so the ratio of G value and R value (G/R) was utilized to reflect the GSH concentration. In Fig. 5b, a satisfactory linear relationship (r
2 = 0.9880) was presented for the G/R value versus the concentration of GSH (1–100 μM). The corresponding linear equation was G/R = −0.00771CGSH +1.39175 and the LOD was calculated to be 0.47 μM based on 3σ/slope. The above results indicated that the portable smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity analysis has great application potential for on-site detection of GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of logic gate device based on dual-fluorescence signals
ce:section.ce:para: CoOOH nanosheets and GSH are two critical conditions affecting the fluorescence signal at 490 nm. Accordingly, a logic gate device with CoOOH nanosheets and GSH as two input signals and F490 and F600 as two output signals was constructed to intelligent semi-quantitative detection of the GSH concentration (Fig. 6 a). For input signals, the absence and existence of GSH or CoOOH nanosheets were specified as “0” and “1”, respectively. Furthermore, the normalized fluorescence intensity at 600 nm was regarded as a constant output signal “1” and the fluorescence intensity at 490 nm was related to the two inputs. As displayed in Fig. 6b, the absence of GSH and presence of CoOOH nanosheets would result in an output of (1, 1), but the presence of high concentration of GSH would lead to the output of (0, 1). Seven different concentrations of GSH ranging from 1 to 100 μM were served as input signals and the input of CoOOH nanosheets was kept at “1”, the obtained normalized fluorescence intensity and corresponding outputs for different GSH concentrations are shown in Fig. 6c and d. For the final outputs of the logic analysis device, when the GSH concentration was 40 μM (adjustable), the corresponding fluorescence intensity at 490 nm was set as the threshold value. The GSH concentration could be reflected by two logic groups: (1, 1) and (0, 1). (1, 1) was outputted when the GSH concentration was less than 40 μM. Otherwise, (0, 1) was generated. Through the constructed logic device, the fluorescence signals were converted into simple binary code to further reflect the GSH levels, which is of great significance for the rapid and intelligent detection of GSH and the preliminary diagnosis of GSH-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility and applicability of the ratiometric fluorescence system for GSH assay in real samples were investigated by using human serum. Standard addition method was used for recovery experiments by spiking different levels of GSH into the diluted serum and the results are exhibited in Table 2 . For ratiometric fluorescence analysis, the recoveries of GSH were in the range of 97.9%–103.8% with the RSDs not exceed 4.5%. For ratiometric chromaticity analysis, the recoveries of GSH ranged from 94.7% to 102.2% with the RSDs not greater than 6.1%. The above results indicated that the ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity analysis have good analytical performance and can be applied for the monitor of GSH in human serum samples.

In this study, a powerful fluorescence sensing analysis platform for GSH assay was constructed using green fluorescent PDA nanoparticles and red fluorescent CPDs with the assistance of CoOOH nanosheets. The CoOOH nanosheets could oxidize DA and the generated PDA nanoparticles emit a green fluorescence at 490 nm. With adding of GSH, CoOOH nanosheets were destroyed, thereby inhibiting the oxidization of DA and resulting in gradually decreased of the fluorescence at 490 nm, while the fluorescence response of system at 600 nm kept almost unchanged, thus achieving accurate detection of GSH by the fluorescence intensity ratio of 490 nm and 600 nm. Besides, the fluorescent color changes from green to red with increasing the GSH concentration. Therefore, a portable smartphone-based sensing platform for GSH on-the-spot detection was developed via using the functions of the chromaticity extraction and processing of smartphone. The GSH concentration could be reflected by the chromaticity ratio of green (G) and red (R) of the fluorescence color image. What's more, an innovative logic gate device was established, which makes it possible to achieve intelligently monitor GSH levels. The constructed sensing analysis platforms could realize the rapid, sensitive and reliable analysis of GSH in human serum, which show great significance for monitoring human health and preliminary disease diagnosis.

Xucan Yuan: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Fujuan Bai: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Software. Heng Ye: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hanqing Zhao: Data curation, Software. Longshan Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Zhili Xiong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020–181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).
